claim: The zodiac section (entropy 3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) uses a label-only encoding scheme where each word is a proper name or fixed label rather than running prose, producing a vocabulary distribution that deviates significantly from Zipf's law relative to other sections.
evidence_for: Zodiac entropy (3.7149) is the lowest of all sections, consistent with a closed label vocabulary
Zodiac has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” very sparse, consistent with repeated labels
Label-dominant text would show a flatter frequency distribution (fewer ultra-high-frequency function words)
Astronomical context strongly implies positional labels (month names, star names, zodiac sign names)
evidence_against: If purely labels, word diversity should be very low, but the section still contributes to the overall 8,261-word unique vocabulary
Currier A/B analysis covers the full manuscript; zodiac section's dialect assignment is unclear
Other label-heavy sections like pharmaceutical also show lower entropy (3.7772), but not as extreme as zodiac
test: Compute per-section Zipf exponent fits independently. Compare zodiac section's Zipf exponent and R-squared to the herbal and text-only sections. Additionally compute type-token ratio (TTR) per section normalized by folio count.
test_metric: Per-section Zipf exponent, R-squared of Zipf fit, and normalized TTR
test_threshold: If zodiac Zipf exponent deviates >0.15 from herbal section and zodiac TTR is >1.5x the herbal TTR per folio, label-encoding hypothesis is supported

tests_run.test: compute per-section zipf exponent fits independently. compare zodiac section's zipf exponent and r-s
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If zodiac encodes a fixed label vocabulary, it can potentially be solved independently of the rest of the manuscript by matching label counts to known zodiac systems, making it a high-value starting point for partial decipherment
